content:
# Numbers Chapter 35 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 193-194 | **Source Image:** `NUM_035_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_035_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. pa_0141-pa_0142

---

**1** And YHWH spoke to Moses in the plains of Moab by the Jordan, opposite Jericho, saying,

**2** "Command the sons of Israel that they give to the Levites, from the inheritance of their possession, cities to dwell in; and pastureland for the cities all around them you shall give to the Levites.

**3** And the cities shall be for them to dwell in, and their pasturelands shall be for their livestock and for their goods and for all their animals.

**4** And the pasturelands of the cities which you shall give to the Levites shall extend from the wall of the city outward one thousand cubits all around.

**5** And you shall measure from outside the city on the east side two thousand cubits, and on the south side two thousand cubits, and on the west side two thousand cubits, and on the north side two thousand cubits, with the city in the middle. This shall be to them the pasturelands of the cities.

**6** And the cities which you shall give to the Levites shall be the six cities of refuge, which you shall give for the manslayer to flee there; and in addition to them you shall give forty-two cities.

**7** All the cities which you shall give to the Levites shall be forty-eight cities — them and their pasturelands.

**8** And the cities which you shall give from the possession of the sons of Israel: from the larger tribe you shall give more, and from the smaller tribe you shall give fewer. Each one according to his inheritance which he inherits shall give of his cities to the Levites."

**9** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

**10** "Speak to the sons of Israel and say to them: When you cross the Jordan into the land of Canaan,

**11** then you shall select for yourselves cities to be cities of refuge for you, that the manslayer who strikes a person unintentionally may flee there.

**12** And the cities shall be for you a refuge from the avenger, so that the manslayer shall not die until he stands before the congregation for judgment.

**13** And the cities which you shall give, six cities of refuge shall be for you.

**14** Three cities you shall give beyond the Jordan, and three cities you shall give in the land of Canaan; cities of refuge shall they be.

**15** For the sons of Israel and for the sojourner and for the resident among them, these six cities shall be for refuge, for anyone who strikes a person unintentionally to flee there.

**16** And if he struck him with an instrument of iron so that he died, he is a murderer; the murderer shall surely be put to death.

**17** And if he struck him with a stone in hand by which one could die, and he died, he is a murderer; the murderer shall surely be put to death.

**18** Or if he struck him with a wooden instrument in hand by which one could die, and he died, he is a murderer; the murderer shall surely be put to death.

**19** The avenger of blood — he himself shall put the murderer to death; when he encounters him, he shall put him to death.

**20** And if he pushed him out of hatred, or hurled something at him with intent, and he died,

**21** or in enmity struck him with his hand, and he died, the one who struck him shall surely be put to death; he is a murderer. The avenger of blood shall put the murderer to death when he encounters him.

**22** But if suddenly, without enmity, he pushed him, or hurled upon him any instrument without intent,

**23** or with any stone by which one could die, without seeing him, and it fell upon him, and he died, and he was not his enemy and was not seeking his harm,

**24** then the congregation shall judge between the striker and the avenger of blood according to these ordinances.

**25** And the congregation shall deliver the manslayer from the hand of the avenger of blood, and the congregation shall return him to his city of refuge to which he had fled; and he shall dwell in it until the death of the high priest who was anointed with the holy oil.

**26** But if the manslayer shall indeed go out beyond the boundary of his city of refuge to which he fled,

**27** and the avenger of blood finds him outside the boundary of his city of refuge, and the avenger of blood kills the manslayer, there is no bloodguilt upon him.

**28** For in his city of refuge he shall dwell until the death of the high priest, and after the death of the high priest the manslayer may return to the land of his possession.

**29** And these things shall be for you as a statute of judgment throughout your generations in all your dwelling places.

**30** Anyone who strikes a person — according to the mouth of witnesses shall the murderer be put to death; but one witness shall not testify against a person to put him to death.

**31** And you shall not take a ransom for the life of a murderer who is guilty of death, for he shall surely be put to death.

**32** And you shall not take a ransom for him who has fled to his city of refuge, to return to dwell in the land before the death of the priest.

**33** And you shall not pollute the land in which you are, for blood — it pollutes the land, and no atonement can be made for the land for the blood that is shed in it, except by the blood of the one who shed it.

**34** And you shall not defile the land in which you dwell, in the midst of which I dwell, for I, YHWH, dwell in the midst of the sons of Israel."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **YHWH (vv. 1, 9, 34):** The Tetragrammaton (יהוה) is transliterated as "YHWH" per LOGOS convention, preserving the divine name as it appears in the Leningrad Codex rather than substituting a title.

2. **"Sons of Israel" (vv. 2, 10, 15, 34):** Hebrew בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל rendered literally as "sons of Israel" rather than the interpretive "Israelites," preserving the patronymic construction.

3. **"Pasturelands" (vv. 2, 3, 4, 5, 7):** Hebrew מִגְרָשׁ (migrash), referring to the open land surrounding Levitical cities designated for grazing. The Vaticanus reads συγκυροῦντα (adjoining lands), but the Hebrew term specifically denotes pasture or common land driven out from the city walls.

4. **"One thousand cubits" / "Two thousand cubits" (vv. 4-5):** The relationship between the one-thousand-cubit measure (v. 4) and the two-thousand-cubit measure (v. 5) has occasioned much discussion. The Leningrad Codex reads אֶלֶף בָּאַמָּה in v. 4 and אַלְפַּיִם בָּאַמָּה in v. 5. The most likely reading takes v. 4 as the pastureland strip and v. 5 as the total measured from the city center, though some interpreters see v. 4 as the pastureland and v. 5 as additional agricultural land. The Vaticanus preserves both measurements (χιλίους and δισχιλίους) without harmonization.

5. **"Manslayer" (vv. 6, 11, 12, 15, 25, 26, 27, 28):** Hebrew רֹצֵחַ (rotseach), the active participle of רצח. This term is broader than "murderer" and encompasses both intentional and unintentional killing. The formal translation renders it "manslayer" in contexts of unintentional killing and "murderer" (vv. 16-19, 21, 30, 31) where the text explicitly identifies intent, following the Hebrew's own distinction.

6. **"Avenger of blood" (vv. 12, 19, 21, 24, 25, 27):** Hebrew גֹּאֵל הַדָּם (go'el haddam). The גֹּאֵל is the kinsman-redeemer, the nearest male relative responsible for avenging the blood of a slain family member. The Vaticanus renders ὁ ἀγχιστεύων τὸ αἷμα, preserving the kinship-redeemer concept.

7. **"Cities of refuge" (vv. 6, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 25, 26, 27, 28, 32):** Hebrew עָרֵי מִקְלָט (arei miqlat). The noun מִקְלָט derives from קלט, "to receive, absorb," thus a place that receives or shelters. The Vaticanus uses πόλεις φυγαδευτήρια (cities of flight/refuge). These six cities (three in Transjordan, three in Canaan proper) form a central institution of Israelite jurisprudence.

8. **"Unintentionally" (vv. 11, 15):** Hebrew בִּשְׁגָגָה (bishgagah), literally "in error" or "by inadvertence." This is a key legal term distinguishing accidental from premeditated homicide, paralleling the distinction in Num 15:22-29. Vaticanus reads ἀκουσίως (involuntarily).

9. **"Instrument of iron" / "stone in hand" / "wooden instrument in hand" (vv. 16-18):** The three categories of lethal weapons — iron (כְלִי בַרְזֶל), stone (אֶבֶן יָד), wood (כְלִי עֵץ יָד) — establish a comprehensive taxonomy of murder weapons. The phrase "in hand" (יָד) with the latter two specifies implements of sufficient size to kill, distinguishing them from incidental contact.

10. **"Without seeing him" (v. 23):** Hebrew בְּלֹא רְאוֹת, a critical legal qualifier indicating that the death was truly accidental — the killer did not even perceive the victim. This clause, together with "without enmity" and "without intent," establishes the full criteria for refuge eligibility.

11. **"Congregation" (vv. 24, 25):** Hebrew עֵדָה (edah), the judicial assembly that adjudicates between the manslayer and the avenger of blood. This is the same term used throughout Numbers for the assembled community of Israel, here functioning in its judicial capacity.

12. **"High priest who was anointed with the holy oil" (v. 25):** Hebrew הַכֹּהֵן הַגָּדוֹל אֲשֶׁר־מָשַׁח אֹתוֹ בְּשֶׁמֶן הַקֹּדֶשׁ. The death of the high priest serves as a kind of expiation or epoch-marker that releases the manslayer from confinement. The Vaticanus reads τοῦ ἱερέως τοῦ μεγάλου ὃν ἔχρισεν αὐτὸν τῷ ἐλαίῳ τῷ ἁγίῳ.

13. **"No bloodguilt upon him" (v. 27):** Hebrew אֵין לוֹ דָּם, literally "there is no blood to him." The avenger incurs no guilt for killing a manslayer found outside the city of refuge. This legal provision underscores that the protection of the refuge city is spatially bounded.

14. **"Ransom" (vv. 31, 32):** Hebrew כֹּפֶר (kopher), a monetary payment to redeem a life. The text emphatically prohibits any financial substitution for capital punishment in cases of murder (v. 31) and any payment to allow early release from the city of refuge (v. 32). This distinguishes Israelite law from other ancient Near Eastern legal systems that permitted pecuniary composition for homicide.

15. **"Pollute" / "Defile" (vv. 33-34):** Two distinct Hebrew verbs: חָנַף (chanaf, "pollute," v. 33) and טָמֵא (tame', "defile," v. 34). The first refers to moral contamination of the land through unavenged bloodshed; the second to ritual impurity. Both verbs underscore the theological principle that the land of YHWH's dwelling must be kept pure. The Vaticanus uses φονοκτονέω (murder/blood-pollute) and μιαίνω (defile), maintaining the distinction.

16. **"For I, YHWH, dwell in the midst of the sons of Israel" (v. 34):** The theological capstone of the chapter. The prohibition against land defilement is grounded not merely in social order but in YHWH's own presence (שֹׁכֵן, shokhein) within Israel. The verb שׁכן (to dwell, tabernacle) connects to the broader Priestly theology of divine habitation among the people (cf. Exod 25:8; 29:45-46).

17. **Conjunctive "And" (throughout):** The Hebrew waw-consecutive and waw-conjunctive are consistently rendered with "And" at the start of verses and clauses, preserving the Hebrew narrative and legal connective structure rather than smoothing it into English prose style.
